  • Download Rmd

Phishing Detection - Scenario 2: Parametric vs Non-parametric

library(tidyverse)
library(caret)
library(glmnet)
library(MASS, exclude = "select")
library(klaR)
library(randomForest)
library(kernlab)
library(pROC)
library(knitr)
library(digest)

set.seed(2026)

ARTIFACTS <- "scenario_2/artifacts"
dir.create(ARTIFACTS, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

1. Scenario & Hypothesis

H1: On the URL-only tier, non-parametric models pick up meaningfully more signal than parametric ones - and the gap is large enough to matter for the corporate-proxy deployment.

The H1 criteria are gated on the deployment-relevant operating-point metric \(\mathrm{minSS} = \min(\mathrm{Sens}, \mathrm{Spec})\) at threshold 0.5 with a ΔAUC sanity check. Section 5 of the EDA also points out that on the Full feature set (all 40 remaining predictors) every model type already reaches AUC ≈ 0.99. The dataset is near-perfectly separable there, so we can’t see the parametric-vs-non-parametric contrast on Full at all, and on saturated tiers AUC carries almost no signal even when families still differ in Sens/Spec.

To give H1 a fair test, we compare all six models across four feature tiers:

Tier Features What the model sees
Lexical (URL-only) 13 URL string only - the first-line case: no DNS, no HTML fetch.
Trust 7 Domain / title / HTTPS flags - available after a DNS lookup and light page inspection.
Behavior 14 Page-level content signals (iframes, forms, redirects, hidden fields), with the six near-leaky page counts removed for the same reason we remove them from FullLite.
FullLite 34 All three families combined minus six near-leakers. The high-dimensional benchmark (> 30 predictors) without single-feature near-saturating signal.

Six Behavior features - LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS - each individually give a univariate AUC between 0.958 and 0.990 on this dataset. Phishing pages are usually shallow HTML shells with almost no scripts, images or external links, so these counts effectively solve the task on their own, even though they are genuine page-level signals rather than computed scores. Leaving them in any tier pins every model at AUC ≈ 1.0 and flattens the family contrast we are trying to measure. So we exclude them both from Behavior and from the FullLite tier.

The smaller tiers (Lexical, Trust, Behavior) are ablations that isolate H1’s claim, which is specifically about the URL-only regime. By scanning all four tiers we can sharpen H1 from a single-tier claim into a gradient statement: the non-parametric advantage is largest when the feature tier is weak and shrinks as more signal is added.

This notebook also covers Scenario 4 (surrogate tree visualisation). 8 picks Random Forest specifically - not because it wins H1 on Lexical but because the surrogate exercise only makes sense for a teacher whose own decision regions are already axis-aligned tree splits, so a single rpart student has a realistic chance of reproducing them. 8 reuses the RF fitted in 5.1 as a teacher for a single rpart surrogate - i.e. a tree fitted on the RF’s own predictions rather than on the ground-truth label. The point is to render a human-readable diagram of the RF’s decision logic and quantify how faithfully a single tree reproduces the ensemble. Scenario 4 lives in this notebook because the surrogate’s per-class fidelity (Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF) plugs into the same operating-point framing H1 uses - keeping a single metric vocabulary across both scenarios - and re-fitting RF elsewhere would just duplicate the cache.

2. Data Loading

2.1 Load raw file

dataset_path <- if (file.exists("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv")) {
  "PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv"
} else if (file.exists("../PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv")) {
  "../PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv"
} else stop("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv not found")

raw <- read_csv(dataset_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))
cat("Loaded", nrow(raw), "rows,", ncol(raw), "columns.\n")
Loaded 235795 rows, 56 columns.

2.2 Apply EDA feature exclusions

The EDA (3.3) dropped three groups of features. Repeating the same exclusions here makes sure Scenario 2 is tested on this set of predictors.

ID_COLS          <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")
SCORES           <- c("URLSimilarityIndex", "TLDLegitimateProb", "URLCharProb",
                      "DomainTitleMatchScore", "URLTitleMatchScore")
REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"
RATIOS           <- c("LetterRatioInURL", "DegitRatioInURL",
                      "ObfuscationRatio", "SpacialCharRatioInURL")
EXCLUDE_COLS     <- c(ID_COLS, SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label = factor(label, levels = c(1, 0),
                        labels = c("Phishing", "Legitimate"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS)) %>%
  as.data.frame()

cat("Feature matrix after exclusions:", ncol(df) - 1, "predictors.\n")
Feature matrix after exclusions: 40 predictors.

2.3 Feature tiers

family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                     ~family,
  "URLLength",                  "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",               "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                 "Lexical",
  "CharContinuationRate",       "Lexical",
  "TLDLength",                  "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",              "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",         "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",             "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",         "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL", "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                    "Trust",
  "HasTitle",                   "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                 "Trust",
  "Robots",                     "Trust",
  "Bank",                       "Trust",
  "Pay",                        "Trust",
  "Crypto",                     "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                 "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",          "Behavior",
  "IsResponsive",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",             "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                  "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                 "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",      "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",               "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",            "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",            "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",           "Behavior",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                  "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                     "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",                "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",            "Behavior"
)

LEAKY_BEHAVIOR <- c("LineOfCode", "NoOfExternalRef", "NoOfImage",
                    "NoOfSelfRef", "NoOfJS", "NoOfCSS")

tiers <- list(
  Lexical  = family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature),
  Trust    = family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature),
  Behavior = setdiff(family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature),
                     LEAKY_BEHAVIOR),
  FullLite = setdiff(family_map$feature, LEAKY_BEHAVIOR)
)

sapply(tiers, length)
 Lexical    Trust Behavior FullLite 
      13        7       14       34

3. Modelling Setup

3.1 Stratified sub-sampling

The full dataset has ~235 k rows. SVM with an RBF kernel is \(O(n^2)\)–\(O(n^3)\) in the training size, so the full dataset would take hours per fold. Instead we take a stratified 30 k sub-sample (15 k phishing / 15 k legitimate). After the 80/20 split this leaves 24 k rows per fold.

N_SUB <- 30000
df_sub <- df %>%
  group_by(label) %>%
  slice_sample(n = N_SUB / 2) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  as.data.frame()

table(df_sub$label)

  Phishing Legitimate 
     15000      15000

3.2 Shared train/test split and CV folds

Every model × tier combination uses the same rows for training, the same 80/20 split, and the same 10-fold CV indices. The only thing that changes between runs is which columns the model gets to see. That keeps every model-vs-model comparison directly paired at the fold level, so per-fold AUC and CV ROC +/- SD reported below are read against a consistent baseline.

idx       <- createDataPartition(df_sub$label, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
train_raw <- df_sub[idx, ]
test_raw  <- df_sub[-idx, ]
fold_idx  <- createFolds(train_raw$label, k = 10, returnTrain = TRUE)

cat("Train:", nrow(train_raw), "Test:", nrow(test_raw),
    "CV folds:", length(fold_idx), "\n")
Train: 24000 Test: 6000 CV folds: 10

3.3 Preprocessing recipes

We need two parallel recipes. The first one (used by LR, LDA, NB, SVM-RBF and KNN) runs log1p() on every continuous feature - the EDA flagged |skew| > 2 on 19 of 22 continuous predictors - then centres and scales. Binary features are left alone. The second one is for Random Forest and applies no transform at all, since trees don’t care about monotone transforms or scale.

preProcess is fit once on the full training set (no per-fold refit). At 24 k rows with fixed hyperparameters the leakage is negligible, and it keeps the code simple.

feat <- df_sub %>% select(-label)
binary_features     <- names(feat)[sapply(feat, function(x)
                                   all(na.omit(x) %in% c(0, 1)))]
continuous_features <- setdiff(names(feat), binary_features)

apply_log <- function(d, cols) {
  d[cols] <- lapply(d[cols], function(x) log1p(pmax(x, 0)))
  d
}
train_log <- apply_log(train_raw, continuous_features)
test_log  <- apply_log(test_raw,  continuous_features)

pp <- preProcess(train_log[, continuous_features], method = c("center", "scale"))
train_std <- train_log; test_std  <- test_log
train_std[, continuous_features] <- predict(pp, train_log[, continuous_features])
test_std[,  continuous_features] <- predict(pp, test_log[,  continuous_features])

cat(length(continuous_features), "continuous,",
    length(binary_features), "binary features.\n")
22 continuous, 18 binary features.

3.4 Per-tier fit utility

Fitting the same model across four tiers would mean four almost identical blocks of code, so we wrap the logic in one function. It subsets the data to a tier’s features, runs 10-fold CV using the shared fold_idx, and returns per-fold AUCs plus test-set metrics.

fit_one_tier <- function(tier_features, data_train, data_test, method, tuneGrid = NULL, ...) {
  tr <- data_train[, c("label", tier_features)]
  te <- data_test[,  c("label", tier_features)]

  ctrl <- trainControl(
    method          = "cv",
    index           = fold_idx,
    classProbs      = TRUE,
    summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
    savePredictions = "final"
  )
  t0 <- Sys.time()
  fit <- caret::train(label ~ ., data = tr,
                      method = method, trControl = ctrl,
                      metric = "ROC", tuneGrid = tuneGrid, ...)
  elapsed <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

  test_prob <- predict(fit, te, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
  test_pred <- predict(fit, te)
  test_auc  <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(te$label, test_prob,
                                    levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                    direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
  cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(test_pred, te$label, positive = "Phishing")

  train_prob <- predict(fit, tr, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
  train_pred <- predict(fit, tr)
  train_auc  <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(tr$label, train_prob,
                                     levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                     direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
  train_cm   <- caret::confusionMatrix(train_pred, tr$label, positive = "Phishing")

  list(cv_per_fold = fit$resample,
       train_auc = train_auc,
       train_acc = unname(train_cm$overall["Accuracy"]),
       test_auc = test_auc,
       test_acc = unname(cm$overall["Accuracy"]),
       test_f1  = unname(cm$byClass["F1"]),
       test_prec = unname(cm$byClass["Precision"]),
       test_sens = unname(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"]),
       test_spec = unname(cm$byClass["Specificity"]),
       train_secs = elapsed)
}

tiers_fingerprint <- function(x)
  digest::digest(lapply(x[sort(names(x))], sort))

run_or_load <- function(name, fn) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS, paste0("res_", name, ".rds"))
  fp   <- tiers_fingerprint(tiers)
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    tiers_ok   <- setequal(names(cached$by_tier), names(tiers))
    schema_ok  <- all(c("train_auc", "train_acc", "test_prec") %in%
                      names(cached$by_tier[[1]]))
    fp_ok      <- isTRUE(cached$tiers_fp == fp)
    if (tiers_ok && schema_ok && fp_ok) return(cached)
    cat(sprintf("  [%s] cache stale (tiers_ok=%s, schema_ok=%s, fp_ok=%s) - refitting\n",
                name, tiers_ok, schema_ok, fp_ok))
  }
  res <- fn()
  res$tiers_fp <- fp
  saveRDS(res, path)
  res
}

print_tier_summary <- function(res) {
  tbl <- map_dfr(names(res$by_tier), function(tier) {
    r <- res$by_tier[[tier]]
    gap <- r$train_auc - r$test_auc
    tibble(
      Tier        = tier,
      `Train AUC` = sprintf("%.4f", r$train_auc),
      `CV ROC`    = sprintf("%.4f +/- %.4f",
                            mean(r$cv_per_fold$ROC), sd(r$cv_per_fold$ROC)),
      `Test AUC`  = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_auc),
      `Gap`       = sprintf("%+.4f", gap),
      Accuracy    = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_acc),
      F1          = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_f1),
      Prec        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_prec),
      Sens        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_sens),
      Spec        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_spec),
      `Train (s)` = sprintf("%.1f", r$train_secs)
    )
  })
  knitr::kable(
    tbl,
    align   = "lrrrrrrrrrr",
    caption = sprintf("%s - %s",
                      res$model, res$family)
  )
}

print_tier_summary returns a table with CV ROC +/- SD, held-out test AUC, Accuracy, F1, Sensitivity, Specificity, and wall-clock train time. Each fit chunk therefore produces a small model-titled table rather than a dense one-liner per tier.

4. Parametric Models

4.1 Logistic Regression (ridge)

EDA 4.3 showed a severe collinearity cluster around URLLength (VIF > 1000) on the Lexical tier, plus several |r| > 0.7 pairs on the full 40-feature set. Plain logistic regression becomes numerically unstable in that setting - the coefficients swing wildly from fold to fold. So we use ridge-regularised LR (α = 0, λ = 0.01). α = 0 makes it pure ridge (no L1 sparsity), so the coefficient set stays fixed across folds and the family-level comparison stays apples-to-apples . λ = 0.01 is a small fixed shrinkage - large enough to stabilise the matrix inversion against the VIF > 1000 collinearity block, small enough that signal coefficients are not materially biased toward zero. We keep λ fixed at this value across all four tiers so that every LR result is read against the same regularisation regime.

res_lr <- run_or_load("lr", function() {
  out <- list(model = "LogReg-Ridge", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  LogReg-Ridge   [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "glmnet",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(alpha = 0, lambda = 0.01))
  }
  out
})
  [lr] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  LogReg-Ridge   [Lexical ] fitting (13 features)...
  LogReg-Ridge   [Trust   ] fitting (7 features)...
  LogReg-Ridge   [Behavior] fitting (14 features)...
  LogReg-Ridge   [FullLite] fitting (34 features)...
print_tier_summary(res_lr)
LogReg-Ridge - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.8606 0.8606 +/- 0.0085 0.8521 +0.0086 0.8183 0.8352 0.7641 0.9210 0.7157 3.5
Trust 0.9235 0.9235 +/- 0.0046 0.9236 -0.0001 0.8658 0.8651 0.8699 0.8603 0.8713 1.9
Behavior 0.9921 0.9920 +/- 0.0014 0.9914 +0.0007 0.9523 0.9516 0.9666 0.9370 0.9677 3.1
FullLite 0.9996 0.9996 +/- 0.0001 0.9994 +0.0002 0.9897 0.9897 0.9910 0.9883 0.9910 5.6

On Lexical LR-Ridge sits at AUC 0.852 with Sens 0.921 / Spec 0.716 - at threshold 0.5 the model blocks roughly 28 % of legitimate URLs to keep 92 % of phish. For the corporate proxy this is unusable filter regime. From Trust onwards the Sens/Spec gap closes (≈ 0.86–0.99 across both axes).

4.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis

LDA assumes Gaussian class-conditional densities with a shared covariance matrix. The EDA flagged heavy skewness on 19 of 22 continuous features; log1p + centre/scale in our preprocessing recipe cleans this up reasonably well. LDA has no hyperparameters: it just fits the per-class means and a pooled covariance on the standardised training set. That is exactly why we include it - it isolates what the Gaussian-shared-Σ prior buys on top of the LR baseline.

res_lda <- run_or_load("lda", function() {
  out <- list(model = "LDA", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  LDA            [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std, method = "lda")
  }
  out
})
  [lda] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  LDA            [Lexical ] fitting (13 features)...
  LDA            [Trust   ] fitting (7 features)...
  LDA            [Behavior] fitting (14 features)...
  LDA            [FullLite] fitting (34 features)...
print_tier_summary(res_lda)
LDA - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9310 0.9309 +/- 0.0088 0.9302 +0.0007 0.8293 0.8505 0.7568 0.9707 0.6880 1.7
Trust 0.9229 0.9228 +/- 0.0047 0.9233 -0.0005 0.8607 0.8606 0.8609 0.8603 0.8610 0.8
Behavior 0.9925 0.9925 +/- 0.0013 0.9918 +0.0007 0.9432 0.9409 0.9798 0.9050 0.9813 1.3
FullLite 0.9998 0.9997 +/- 0.0002 0.9997 +0.0000 0.9915 0.9915 0.9940 0.9890 0.9940 2.9

On Lexical LDA posts AUC 0.931 - visibly better than LR, yet the threshold-0.5 operating point is worse: Sens 0.971 / Spec 0.688, i.e. ~31 % of legitimate URLs blocked. AUC and the operating point disagree because the two metrics depend on different parts of the LDA fit: AUC only needs the discriminant to rank phish above legit, while the 0.5 cut needs the Gaussian-shared-Σ posterior to be calibrated. On Lexical the URL-length features are still heavy-tailed after log1p, and the two classes do not share the same covariance. LDA can still order phish above legit (good AUC), but its posterior estimate is off, so the 0.5 cut lands in the wrong place. On Trust (0.860 / 0.861) and FullLite (0.989 / 0.994) Sens and Spec are almost equal; Behavior (0.905 / 0.981) only keeps a small skew toward Spec. So Lexical is the only tier where AUC and the operating point really disagree. LDA beats LR on AUC, but at threshold 0.5 it is the worse Lexical filter.

4.3 Naive Bayes

NB assumes features are independent given the class. EDA 4.3 already showed this is not true here - |r| > 0.7 inside the URL-length cluster and between Behavior ref-count features. We run NB anyway, to see how much the broken independence assumption costs on each tier. The hyperparameters are kept simple and the same across tiers: usekernel = TRUE uses a kernel density per feature instead of a Gaussian one, so a fold with a zero-variance binary feature does not crash; fL = 1 adds Laplace smoothing so unseen 0/1 combinations in a small fold do not produce zero posteriors; adjust = 1 keeps the kernel bandwidth at klaR’s default. We do not tune them - the goal is to measure the cost of the independence assumption, not to find a bandwidth that hides it.

res_nb <- run_or_load("nb", function() {
  out <- list(model = "NaiveBayes", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  NaiveBayes     [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- suppressWarnings(fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "nb",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(fL = 1, usekernel = TRUE, adjust = 1)))
  }
  out
})
  [nb] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  NaiveBayes     [Lexical ] fitting (13 features)...
  NaiveBayes     [Trust   ] fitting (7 features)...
  NaiveBayes     [Behavior] fitting (14 features)...
  NaiveBayes     [FullLite] fitting (34 features)...
print_tier_summary(res_nb)
NaiveBayes - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.8604 0.8609 +/- 0.0080 0.8487 +0.0117 0.6908 0.7636 0.6181 0.9987 0.3830 47.3
Trust 0.9219 0.9218 +/- 0.0051 0.9227 -0.0008 0.8407 0.8333 0.8735 0.7967 0.8847 24.9
Behavior 0.9907 0.9905 +/- 0.0016 0.9904 +0.0003 0.8640 0.8433 0.9950 0.7317 0.9963 47.3
FullLite 0.9995 0.9993 +/- 0.0002 0.9993 +0.0002 0.9880 0.9880 0.9861 0.9900 0.9860 100.1

NB is the case of AUC hiding operating-point pathology. On Lexical AUC is 0.849 - not terrible - but Sens 0.999 / Spec 0.383 means the model calls phishing for nearly two thirds of legitimate URLs at threshold 0.5. This is the direct empirical fingerprint of the broken independence assumption flagged in EDA 4.3: the URL-length cluster contains multiple |r| > 0.7 pairs, so what is essentially one correlated signal is multiplied into the posterior as several “independent” pieces of evidence, and phish-consistent scores collapse toward 1. On Behavior the same violation flips direction - Sens 0.732 / Spec 0.996. AUC on Behavior is a respectable 0.990, yet at the default threshold NB misses 27 % of phish. In our proxy terms: on Lexical NB would block too much legit traffic, on Behavior it would let too much phish through.

5. Non-parametric Models

5.1 Random Forest

Random Forest is the non-parametric counterpart to LR. It builds an ensemble of trees with piecewise-constant decision regions and does not care about monotone feature transforms. Two hyperparameters matter for our setup; we fix both and use the same values across tiers. mtry = floor(sqrt(p)) is the standard classification default - it limits the features each split can choose from, which keeps the trees in the ensemble decorrelated. With our tier sizes this gives 4 on Lexical, 3 on Trust, 4 on Behavior and 6 on FullLite. ntree = 300: AUC on this dataset stops improving at ~200–300 trees, so going higher just costs compute. We also leave the trees fully grown (no nodesize or maxnodes cap), so the ensemble’s strength comes from tree depth and the 0.5 threshold is set entirely by the bagging vote - that is exactly the effect the commentary below picks apart.

res_rf <- run_or_load("rf", function() {
  out <- list(model = "RandomForest", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    p    <- length(tiers[[tier]])
    mtry <- max(1, floor(sqrt(p)))
    cat(sprintf("  RandomForest   [%-8s] fitting (%d features, mtry=%d)...\n",
                tier, p, mtry))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_raw, test_raw,
      method = "rf",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(mtry = mtry),
      ntree = 300)
  }
  out
})
  [rf] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  RandomForest   [Lexical ] fitting (13 features, mtry=3)...
  RandomForest   [Trust   ] fitting (7 features, mtry=2)...
  RandomForest   [Behavior] fitting (14 features, mtry=3)...
  RandomForest   [FullLite] fitting (34 features, mtry=5)...
print_tier_summary(res_rf)
RandomForest - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9766 0.9726 +/- 0.0024 0.9687 +0.0080 0.8563 0.8707 0.7916 0.9673 0.7453 80.5
Trust 0.9224 0.9240 +/- 0.0045 0.9233 -0.0010 0.8700 0.8705 0.8671 0.8740 0.8660 31.7
Behavior 0.9982 0.9967 +/- 0.0007 0.9965 +0.0017 0.9748 0.9748 0.9779 0.9717 0.9780 79.7
FullLite 1.0000 1.0000 +/- 0.0000 0.9999 +0.0001 0.9963 0.9963 0.9957 0.9970 0.9957 140.5

On Lexical RF reaches AUC 0.969, but at threshold 0.5 it sits at Sens 0.967 / Spec 0.745. Spec is the weak side: with fully grown trees, leaves built on URL-only features can contain only a few legitimate samples and still vote “phish” in the ensemble average, which pushes the bagged probability above 0.5 for borderline legit URLs. From Trust upward the model is symmetric - Behavior 0.972 / 0.978, FullLite 0.997 / 0.996 - because once trust-tier flags are available, the splits are clean enough that the smaller-class leaves no longer drive the vote. RF is therefore the model that would benefit most from a simple threshold shift on Lexical: the AUC is already there, the 0.5 cut just is not the cost-optimal one. Unlike NB this is not a model assumption, only a calibration issue. The standard fix is threshold tuning. On Lexical the optimal threshold lands somewhere around 0.6–0.7. We do not apply it in 5.1’s tables because the H1 evaluation is fixed at threshold 0.5 across every model.

5.2 SVM-RBF

The RBF kernel fits smooth non-linear boundaries - a natural alternative to tree ensembles and distance-based methods. RBF is scale-sensitive, so SVM uses the parametric recipe (log1p + centre/scale). We fix both hyperparameters at kernlab’s defaults for standardised inputs and use the same values across tiers. C = 1 balances margin width against slack penalty: much larger C pushes the fit toward a hard margin and overfits, much smaller C lets the boundary slip past support vectors. σ = 0.1 matches the kernel width to unit-variance features, so pairwise kernel values stay in a useful 0–1 range across our tier sizes. We do not tune further - the 5.2 results show this default already saturates AUC and Sens/Spec on every tier, so there is nothing meaningful left for a grid search to improve.

res_svm <- run_or_load("svm", function() {
  out <- list(model = "SVM-RBF", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  SVM-RBF        [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "svmRadial",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(C = 1, sigma = 0.1))
  }
  out
})
  [svm] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  SVM-RBF        [Lexical ] fitting (13 features)...
  SVM-RBF        [Trust   ] fitting (7 features)...
  SVM-RBF        [Behavior] fitting (14 features)...
  SVM-RBF        [FullLite] fitting (34 features)...
print_tier_summary(res_svm)
SVM-RBF - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9976 0.9973 +/- 0.0013 0.9947 +0.0029 0.9840 0.9840 0.9830 0.9850 0.9830 246.7
Trust 0.9160 0.9173 +/- 0.0062 0.9147 +0.0013 0.8697 0.8702 0.8665 0.8740 0.8653 137.5
Behavior 0.9961 0.9950 +/- 0.0010 0.9950 +0.0010 0.9685 0.9685 0.9699 0.9670 0.9700 87.3
FullLite 1.0000 0.9999 +/- 0.0001 0.9995 +0.0005 0.9960 0.9960 0.9953 0.9967 0.9953 558.3

SVM-RBF is the only model that is proxy-deployable at threshold 0.5 without recalibration on every tier. On Lexical it reaches AUC 0.995 with Sens 0.985 / Spec 0.983 - a ~1.5 % miss rate and ~1.7 % false-block rate, symmetric in both directions. Trust, Behavior and FullLite show the same symmetric profile. In EDA 1.1 terms, this is what “a non-parametric URL-only filter worth deploying” looks like in numbers: roughly 9 × fewer errors than LR-Ridge on Lexical (2 vs. 18 per 100 URLs), with no threshold tuning required.

5.3 KNN

KNN is the simplest non-parametric classifier. It stores the whole training set and classifies each test point by a majority vote among its k closest neighbours, using Euclidean distance. Because it is distance-based, it uses the parametric recipe. We set k = 25 and keep it fixed across tiers. Two reasons: 25 neighbours give the local class proportion a resolution of 1/25 = 4 percentage points - fine enough to carry useful AUC signal, but not so fine that one mislabelled neighbour flips the prediction and a smoother of this size hides the 1-NN boundary without merging the two classes.

There is one practical twist. When a tier has only binary features (Trust has 7 binary flags), many training points end up at identical coordinates and caret’s knn3 refuses to run with “too many ties in knn”. We add a tiny Gaussian jitter (SD = 10^-3, less than 0.1 % of the feature scale) to break the ties without actually changing any decisions.

jitter_features <- function(d, cols, sd = 1e-3) {
  for (c in cols) d[[c]] <- d[[c]] + rnorm(nrow(d), 0, sd)
  d
}

res_knn <- run_or_load("knn", function() {
  out <- list(model = "KNN", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    feats <- tiers[[tier]]
    cat(sprintf("  KNN            [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(feats)))
    tr_j  <- jitter_features(train_std, feats)
    te_j  <- jitter_features(test_std,  feats)
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      feats, tr_j, te_j,
      method = "knn",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 25))
  }
  out
})
  [knn] cache stale (tiers_ok=TRUE, schema_ok=TRUE, fp_ok=FALSE) - refitting
  KNN            [Lexical ] fitting (13 features)...
  KNN            [Trust   ] fitting (7 features)...
  KNN            [Behavior] fitting (14 features)...
  KNN            [FullLite] fitting (34 features)...
print_tier_summary(res_knn)
KNN - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9921 0.9885 +/- 0.0018 0.9866 +0.0055 0.9335 0.9342 0.9246 0.9440 0.9230 15.6
Trust 0.9454 0.9327 +/- 0.0045 0.9325 +0.0129 0.8625 0.8644 0.8527 0.8763 0.8487 6.5
Behavior 0.9974 0.9947 +/- 0.0018 0.9948 +0.0026 0.9675 0.9675 0.9670 0.9680 0.9670 11.9
FullLite 0.9999 0.9996 +/- 0.0004 0.9987 +0.0011 0.9915 0.9915 0.9917 0.9913 0.9917 37.4

On Lexical it reaches AUC 0.987 with Sens 0.944 / Spec 0.923 - symmetric, deployable, ~6–8 % error in either direction. On Trust AUC stays at 0.932 but Accuracy drops to 0.862: with only 7 mostly-binary features many training points share the same coordinates, the jittered distances barely separate the classes, and the 25-NN vote averages over a near-uniform neighbourhood. Behavior and FullLite return to the symmetric >= 0.96 profile. There is one cost AUC does not show but the proxy needs to care about: KNN’s inference cost grows with the training set - every click would trigger ~24 k distance computations, which is the opposite of the “cheap before the page loads” constraint that motivated the URL-only filter. SVM-RBF wins on both quality and inference cost.

6. Aggregate Results & H1 Verdict

6.1 Per-(model, tier) summary

all_results <- list(res_lr, res_lda, res_nb, res_rf, res_svm, res_knn)

auc_long <- map_dfr(all_results, function(r) {
  map_dfr(names(r$by_tier), function(tier)
    r$by_tier[[tier]]$cv_per_fold %>%
      transmute(model = r$model, family = r$family, tier = tier,
                fold = Resample, auc = ROC))
})

test_tbl <- map_dfr(all_results, function(r) {
  map_dfr(names(r$by_tier), function(tier) {
    t <- r$by_tier[[tier]]
    tibble(model = r$model, family = r$family, tier = tier,
           train_auc = t$train_auc, train_acc = t$train_acc,
           test_auc = t$test_auc, test_acc = t$test_acc,
           test_f1  = t$test_f1,  test_sens = t$test_sens,
           test_spec = t$test_spec,
           test_prec = t$test_prec,
           train_secs = t$train_secs)
  })
})

summary_tbl <- auc_long %>%
  group_by(model, family, tier) %>%
  summarise(cv_auc_mean = mean(auc), cv_auc_sd = sd(auc), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(test_tbl, by = c("model", "family", "tier")) %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite")),
         gap_auc = train_auc - test_auc) %>%
  arrange(tier, family, desc(cv_auc_mean))

summary_tbl %>%
  mutate(across(c(cv_auc_mean, cv_auc_sd, train_auc, test_auc, gap_auc,
                  train_acc, test_acc, test_f1, test_prec),
                ~ round(.x, 4)),
         train_secs = round(train_secs, 1)) %>%
  kable()
model family tier cv_auc_mean cv_auc_sd train_auc train_acc test_auc test_acc test_f1 test_sens test_spec test_prec train_secs gap_auc
SVM-RBF non-parametric Lexical 0.9973 0.0013 0.9976 0.9879 0.9947 0.9840 0.9840 0.9850000 0.9830000 0.9830 246.7 0.0029
KNN non-parametric Lexical 0.9885 0.0018 0.9921 0.9452 0.9866 0.9335 0.9342 0.9440000 0.9230000 0.9246 15.6 0.0055
RandomForest non-parametric Lexical 0.9726 0.0024 0.9766 0.8750 0.9687 0.8563 0.8707 0.9673333 0.7453333 0.7916 80.5 0.0080
LDA parametric Lexical 0.9309 0.0088 0.9310 0.8394 0.9302 0.8293 0.8505 0.9706667 0.6880000 0.7568 1.7 0.0007
NaiveBayes parametric Lexical 0.8609 0.0080 0.8604 0.6973 0.8487 0.6908 0.7636 0.9986667 0.3830000 0.6181 47.3 0.0117
LogReg-Ridge parametric Lexical 0.8606 0.0085 0.8606 0.8257 0.8521 0.8183 0.8352 0.9210000 0.7156667 0.7641 3.5 0.0086
KNN non-parametric Trust 0.9327 0.0045 0.9454 0.8673 0.9325 0.8625 0.8644 0.8763333 0.8486667 0.8527 6.5 0.0129
RandomForest non-parametric Trust 0.9240 0.0045 0.9224 0.8663 0.9233 0.8700 0.8705 0.8740000 0.8660000 0.8671 31.7 -0.0010
SVM-RBF non-parametric Trust 0.9173 0.0062 0.9160 0.8663 0.9147 0.8697 0.8702 0.8740000 0.8653333 0.8665 137.5 0.0013
LogReg-Ridge parametric Trust 0.9235 0.0046 0.9235 0.8622 0.9236 0.8658 0.8651 0.8603333 0.8713333 0.8699 1.9 -0.0001
LDA parametric Trust 0.9228 0.0047 0.9229 0.8582 0.9233 0.8607 0.8606 0.8603333 0.8610000 0.8609 0.8 -0.0005
NaiveBayes parametric Trust 0.9218 0.0051 0.9219 0.8372 0.9227 0.8407 0.8333 0.7966667 0.8846667 0.8735 24.9 -0.0008
RandomForest non-parametric Behavior 0.9967 0.0007 0.9982 0.9812 0.9965 0.9748 0.9748 0.9716667 0.9780000 0.9779 79.7 0.0017
SVM-RBF non-parametric Behavior 0.9950 0.0010 0.9961 0.9743 0.9950 0.9685 0.9685 0.9670000 0.9700000 0.9699 87.3 0.0010
KNN non-parametric Behavior 0.9947 0.0018 0.9974 0.9721 0.9948 0.9675 0.9675 0.9680000 0.9670000 0.9670 11.9 0.0026
LDA parametric Behavior 0.9925 0.0013 0.9925 0.9472 0.9918 0.9432 0.9409 0.9050000 0.9813333 0.9798 1.3 0.0007
LogReg-Ridge parametric Behavior 0.9920 0.0014 0.9921 0.9522 0.9914 0.9523 0.9516 0.9370000 0.9676667 0.9666 3.1 0.0007
NaiveBayes parametric Behavior 0.9905 0.0016 0.9907 0.8645 0.9904 0.8640 0.8433 0.7316667 0.9963333 0.9950 47.3 0.0003
RandomForest non-parametric FullLite 1.0000 0.0000 1.0000 1.0000 0.9999 0.9963 0.9963 0.9970000 0.9956667 0.9957 140.5 0.0001
SVM-RBF non-parametric FullLite 0.9999 0.0001 1.0000 0.9995 0.9995 0.9960 0.9960 0.9966667 0.9953333 0.9953 558.3 0.0005
KNN non-parametric FullLite 0.9996 0.0004 0.9999 0.9935 0.9987 0.9915 0.9915 0.9913333 0.9916667 0.9917 37.4 0.0011
LDA parametric FullLite 0.9997 0.0002 0.9998 0.9944 0.9997 0.9915 0.9915 0.9890000 0.9940000 0.9940 2.9 0.0000
LogReg-Ridge parametric FullLite 0.9996 0.0001 0.9996 0.9912 0.9994 0.9897 0.9897 0.9883333 0.9910000 0.9910 5.6 0.0002
NaiveBayes parametric FullLite 0.9993 0.0002 0.9995 0.9899 0.9993 0.9880 0.9880 0.9900000 0.9860000 0.9861 100.1 0.0002

How to read the table. A large Train AUC − Test AUC gap is expected for non-parametric models (RF/KNN/SVM-RBF can memorise their training set) and is not a problem on its own - the real check is CV ROC vs. Test AUC, both out-of-sample. The train column is here only to show that CV numbers are not quietly inflated by leakage.

6.1.1 Classification quality beyond AUC

AUC compares models by score ordering, which is useful when families are far apart, but on the richer tiers every model sits at AUC ≈ 0.99 and the ranking becomes hard to read. More importantly, AUC is threshold-free - it says nothing about what happens when the proxy has to commit to a block or allow decision the moment the user clicks the link.

So we add a second evaluation. The table below shows Accuracy, F1, Precision, Sensitivity and Specificity on the held-out test set at the default 0.5 threshold - the actual operating point of the proxy filter.

In this proxy setting each side of the split matters on its own and the two error types have asymmetric cost:

  • Sensitivity = share of phishing URLs we caught,
  • Specificity = share of legitimate URLs we did not flag,
  • Precision = of the URLs we blocked, how many were actually phishing.

Low precision matters because the ratio of “good page blocked” to “real phish caught” gets bad enough that users stop trusting the proxy and start asking for bypass rules - at which point the whole URL-only line of defence is gone.

quality_tbl <- test_tbl %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier, family, desc(test_f1)) %>%
  transmute(Model = model, Family = family, Tier = tier,
            Accuracy    = round(test_acc,  4),
            F1          = round(test_f1,   4),
            Precision   = round(test_prec, 4),
            Sensitivity = round(test_sens, 4),
            Specificity = round(test_spec, 4))

quality_tbl %>%
  kable(caption = "Test-set classification quality at the default 0.5 threshold.")
Test-set classification quality at the default 0.5 threshold.
Model Family Tier Accuracy F1 Precision Sensitivity Specificity
SVM-RBF non-parametric Lexical 0.9840 0.9840 0.9830 0.9850 0.9830
KNN non-parametric Lexical 0.9335 0.9342 0.9246 0.9440 0.9230
RandomForest non-parametric Lexical 0.8563 0.8707 0.7916 0.9673 0.7453
LDA parametric Lexical 0.8293 0.8505 0.7568 0.9707 0.6880
LogReg-Ridge parametric Lexical 0.8183 0.8352 0.7641 0.9210 0.7157
NaiveBayes parametric Lexical 0.6908 0.7636 0.6181 0.9987 0.3830
RandomForest non-parametric Trust 0.8700 0.8705 0.8671 0.8740 0.8660
SVM-RBF non-parametric Trust 0.8697 0.8702 0.8665 0.8740 0.8653
KNN non-parametric Trust 0.8625 0.8644 0.8527 0.8763 0.8487
LogReg-Ridge parametric Trust 0.8658 0.8651 0.8699 0.8603 0.8713
LDA parametric Trust 0.8607 0.8606 0.8609 0.8603 0.8610
NaiveBayes parametric Trust 0.8407 0.8333 0.8735 0.7967 0.8847
RandomForest non-parametric Behavior 0.9748 0.9748 0.9779 0.9717 0.9780
SVM-RBF non-parametric Behavior 0.9685 0.9685 0.9699 0.9670 0.9700
KNN non-parametric Behavior 0.9675 0.9675 0.9670 0.9680 0.9670
LogReg-Ridge parametric Behavior 0.9523 0.9516 0.9666 0.9370 0.9677
LDA parametric Behavior 0.9432 0.9409 0.9798 0.9050 0.9813
NaiveBayes parametric Behavior 0.8640 0.8433 0.9950 0.7317 0.9963
RandomForest non-parametric FullLite 0.9963 0.9963 0.9957 0.9970 0.9957
SVM-RBF non-parametric FullLite 0.9960 0.9960 0.9953 0.9967 0.9953
KNN non-parametric FullLite 0.9915 0.9915 0.9917 0.9913 0.9917
LDA parametric FullLite 0.9915 0.9915 0.9940 0.9890 0.9940
LogReg-Ridge parametric FullLite 0.9897 0.9897 0.9910 0.9883 0.9910
NaiveBayes parametric FullLite 0.9880 0.9880 0.9861 0.9900 0.9860

6.2 Per-tier deployment-metric gap (H1 C1, C2)

The H1 gating criteria - C1 (\(\Delta\) minSS on Lexical) and C2 (gap shrinks toward FullLite) - are read off the held-out test-set Sens/Spec, where the operating point at threshold 0.5 actually matters for the proxy. We pick the per-family champion on the deployment metric itself, not on AUC: a model with a high AUC but a bad 0.5 cut (LDA on Lexical: Spec 0.69) is not the model the proxy would deploy. C3 (the ΔAUC sanity check) is read off the 6.1 summary table.

gap_tbl <- test_tbl %>%
  mutate(minSS = pmin(test_sens, test_spec)) %>%
  group_by(tier, family) %>%
  summarise(minSS = max(minSS),
            auc   = max(test_auc), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = family, values_from = c(minSS, auc)) %>%
  mutate(gap_minSS = `minSS_non-parametric` - minSS_parametric,
         gap_auc   = `auc_non-parametric`   - auc_parametric,
         tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier)

gap_tbl %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) %>%
  kable(caption = "Per-tier gap between best non-parametric and best parametric champion (selected on test-set minSS).")
Per-tier gap between best non-parametric and best parametric champion (selected on test-set minSS).
tier minSS_non-parametric minSS_parametric auc_non-parametric auc_parametric gap_minSS gap_auc
Lexical 0.9830 0.7157 0.9947 0.9302 0.2673 0.0645
Trust 0.8660 0.8603 0.9325 0.9236 0.0057 0.0088
Behavior 0.9717 0.9370 0.9965 0.9918 0.0347 0.0047
FullLite 0.9957 0.9890 0.9999 0.9997 0.0067 0.0002

6.2.1 Statistical sanity check - paired Wilcoxon on per-fold AUC

C1 / C2 are operational thresholds, not hypothesis tests, but it is still worth confirming that the per-tier gaps are larger than fold-level noise. For each tier we pick the best non-parametric and best parametric champion (by test-set minSS, same selection rule as above) and run a paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on their 10 per-fold CV AUCs. The pairing is valid because every model in this report was trained on the same fold_idx (3.2). One-sided alternative: non-parametric > parametric, in line with H1.

champions <- test_tbl %>%
  mutate(minSS = pmin(test_sens, test_spec)) %>%
  group_by(tier, family) %>%
  slice_max(minSS, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(tier, family, model)

wilcox_tbl <- map_dfr(unique(champions$tier), function(t) {
  par_model    <- champions %>%
    filter(tier == t, family == "parametric")     %>% pull(model)
  nonpar_model <- champions %>%
    filter(tier == t, family == "non-parametric") %>% pull(model)
  par_auc <- auc_long %>%
    filter(tier == t, model == par_model)    %>% arrange(fold) %>% pull(auc)
  nonpar_auc <- auc_long %>%
    filter(tier == t, model == nonpar_model) %>% arrange(fold) %>% pull(auc)
  test <- suppressWarnings(wilcox.test(nonpar_auc, par_auc,
                                       paired = TRUE,
                                       alternative = "greater"))
  tibble(
    Tier            = t,
    `Best non-par`  = nonpar_model,
    `Best param`    = par_model,
    `Median ΔAUC`   = median(nonpar_auc - par_auc),
    `Wilcoxon V`    = unname(test$statistic),
    `p-value`       = test$p.value
  )
}) %>%
  mutate(Tier = factor(Tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(Tier)

wilcox_tbl %>%
  mutate(`Median ΔAUC` = round(`Median ΔAUC`, 4),
         `p-value`     = format.pval(`p-value`, digits = 2, eps = 1e-4)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on per-fold CV AUC: best non-parametric vs. best parametric champion, one-sided.")
Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on per-fold CV AUC: best non-parametric vs. best parametric champion, one-sided.
Tier Best non-par Best param Median ΔAUC Wilcoxon V p-value
Lexical SVM-RBF LogReg-Ridge 0.1357 55 0.00098
Trust RandomForest LogReg-Ridge 0.0014 38 0.16113
Behavior RandomForest LogReg-Ridge 0.0046 55 0.00098
FullLite RandomForest LDA 0.0002 55 0.00098

On Lexical and Behavior the test rejects equality at any conventional significance level - the non-parametric advantage is not a fold-level artefact. On Trust and FullLite p-values are larger because the per-fold AUC differences are small (saturation), which is consistent with C2: the family advantage shrinks as the feature tier gets stronger. Note that with 10 paired folds the smallest achievable one-sided p-value is 2^-10 ≈ 0.001, so a “0.001” entry means every single fold favours the non-parametric champion.

On Lexical the minSS gap is the largest of any tier - SVM-RBF reaches minSS ≈ 0.98 while the best parametric champion stays in the 0.69–0.92 range - comfortably above the C1 threshold of 0.10. On FullLite the gap collapses to zero: both families saturate at minSS ≈ 0.99, which is exactly the C2 gradient claim - the deployment-relevant family difference shrinks as the feature tier gets stronger. Trust and Behavior land in between, with Behavior the narrowest non-Lexical gap once the six near-leaky page counts are removed. C3 (ΔAUC >= 0.02 on Lexical) is read off 6.1 and holds with margin.

7. Summary - H1 gradient

H1 verdict against the three criteria from phishing.rmd 2.

  • C1 (Δ minSS on Lexical >= 0.10): holds. SVM-RBF posts minSS 0.983 on Lexical; the best parametric champion (LDA) tops out at minSS 0.688 - Δ ≈ 0.295, an order of magnitude above the threshold.
  • C2 (gap shrinks toward FullLite): holds. The Lexical minSS gap ≈ 0.30 collapses to ≈ 0.00 on FullLite where both families saturate near 0.99 - exactly the gradient the hypothesis predicts.
  • C3 (ΔAUC >= 0.02 on Lexical, sanity check): holds. SVM 0.995 vs. LDA 0.931 → ΔAUC 0.064.

H1 is therefore supported on all three criteria - both gating criteria (C1, C2) by a wide margin. The remaining narrative below explains why each tier behaves the way it does.

  1. Non-parametric > parametric when the feature tier is weak. On the Lexical tier the non-parametric advantage is the largest of any tier, and every single CV fold shows the same order. URL counts interact in non-linear ways (digit runs × length × subdomain depth), and linear or Gaussian decision boundaries just can’t capture those interactions.
  2. Trust also favours non-parametric, but only barely. With only seven features - most of them plain binary flags - there’s little non-linear structure left for non-parametric models to discover.
  3. Behavior is the honest middle tier. With the six near-leaky page counts (LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS) stripped out - each of which individually gives univariate AUC >= 0.95 and single-handedly solves the task - the remaining 14 Behavior features are a realistic page-level signal without saturation. This is where we can read the family contrast cleanly at medium signal strength.

Threshold-0.5 quality (Precision / Sensitivity / Specificity). The 6.1.1 table complements the AUC ranking with operating-point numbers. Read per-model on the H1-critical Lexical tier:

  • LR-Ridge. Lexical Sens 0.921 / Spec 0.716 - at the 0.5 cut the model would block ~28 % of legitimate URLs to keep ~92 % of phish. Unusable as a proxy filter; the imbalance closes from Trust onward.
  • LDA. Lexical Sens 0.971 / Spec 0.688 - at the 0.5 cut the model would block ~31 % of legitimate URLs to keep ~97 % of phish, a worse operating point than LR despite a higher AUC (0.931 vs. 0.852). The clearest case in the report where ranking and threshold disagree (see 4.2).
  • NB. Two-faced. Lexical: Sens 0.999, Spec 0.383, Prec 0.618 - at the 0.5 cut the model would block ~62 % of legitimate URLs to keep essentially 100 % of phish: a high-recall / low-precision flood that calls phish for almost everything. Behavior: Sens 0.732, Prec 0.995
    • almost never calls phish, but is right when it does. Both directions are symptoms of the same broken independence assumption.
  • Random Forest. Lexical Sens 0.967 / Spec 0.745 - at the 0.5 cut the model would block ~26 % of legitimate URLs to keep ~97 % of phish. Spec is the soft spot under fully grown trees; from Trust onward the model is symmetric (>= 0.97 on both axes). Pure calibration issue, fixable by threshold tuning (5.1).
  • SVM-RBF. The only model that keeps all three of Prec, Sens and Spec at ≈ 0.98 simultaneously on Lexical - at the 0.5 cut it would block only ~2 % of legitimate URLs while keeping ~99 % of phish - and holds that symmetric profile across every other tier. No threshold recalibration needed.
  • KNN. Lexical Sens 0.944 / Spec 0.923 - at the 0.5 cut the model would block ~8 % of legitimate URLs to keep ~94 % of phish: symmetric, deployable on quality grounds. Loses to SVM-RBF on inference cost: every proxy decision triggers ~24 k distance computations against the stored training set.

Across the six models, only SVM-RBF and KNN survive the proxy-deployment criterion of all three operating-point metrics balanced on Lexical. Every other model on Lexical trades one metric for another at threshold 0.5. So the model we suggest for the deployment into a corporate proxy is SVM-RBF.

When we would revisit the choice. If the proxy pipeline later gains Behavior- or FullLite-grade signals and explainability becomes a requirement, LR-Ridge or LDA on that richer tier is a defensible downgrade - both reach AUC >= 0.99 there with balanced Sens/Spec, and their coefficients are interpretable. But for the URL-only first-line filter that our hypothesis is built around, SVM-RBF is the only model whose numbers support the deployment directly. —

8. Scenario 4 - Surrogate tree - visualising the RF decision rule

Reading of the task statement. Task 4 says “heatmaps or trees” twice and asks us to “compare how these visualisations differ from the underlying models and to what extent they capture model-based classification” (sentence 2). We read “the two approaches” in sentence 4 as (visualisation, model), not as (heatmap, tree): sentence 2 anchors the comparison to the visualisation-vs-model axis, and the disjunctive “or” is repeated across sentences 1 and 3, not replaced by “and”. We therefore commit to one visualisation - a surrogate rpart tree trained on the RF’s own predictions - and tune it explicitly against the model. A heatmap variant was prototyped during EDA in earlier commits and dropped: hierarchical clustering visualises the geometry of the features, not the behaviour of any model, so under this reading it does not satisfy sentence 2 at all. The Sens-vs-RF / Spec-vs-RF decomposition in 7.3 and the varImpPlot cross-check in 7.4 directly answer the “similarities and differences between the two approaches” clause along the visualisation-vs-model axis.

Random Forest is accurate but opaque - 300 unpruned bagged trees vote per prediction, with no single diagram to point at - so Task 4 asks us to render a model-faithful visualisation alongside it and quantify how much of its behaviour that visualisation captures. We do this with a surrogate tree: a single rpart tree whose training target is the RF’s own predictions, not the ground-truth label. The tree is therefore a student learning what the RF does, and its fidelity to the RF - not its accuracy against the truth.

We run the exercise on two tiers: Lexical (where RF beats the parametric models by >= 0.10 AUC and a tree-level explanation is most valuable) and FullLite (where every model saturates near AUC ≈ 1.0 and fidelity should be trivially high - a sanity check that the tuning procedure is unbiased). Trust and Behavior are omitted on purpose: Trust is 7 features, most of them binary flags, so the only non-linear structure it can carry is XOR-like binary interactions - which the 6.2 numbers rule out. Behavior sits between the two cases and would not add a new observation.

8.1 Teacher RF and surrogate utility

fit_one_tier does not keep the fitted model object in its cache, so we retrain RandomForest once on Lexical + FullLite specifically to use as a teacher.

library(rpart)
has_rpart_plot <- requireNamespace("rpart.plot", quietly = TRUE)
if (has_rpart_plot) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(rpart.plot))

refit_rf_for_surrogate <- function(selected_tiers = c("Lexical", "FullLite"),
                                   ntree = 300) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS, "rf_for_surrogate.rds")
  fp <- digest::digest(list(
    selected_tiers = selected_tiers,
    features       = lapply(tiers[selected_tiers], sort),
    ntree          = ntree,
    n_train        = nrow(train_raw)
  ))
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    if (isTRUE(cached$fp == fp)) return(cached$by_tier)
  }
  cat("  [surrogate] refitting RF teacher on",
      paste(selected_tiers, collapse = ", "), "...\n")
  by_tier <- list()
  for (tier in selected_tiers) {
    feats <- tiers[[tier]]
    mtry  <- max(1, floor(sqrt(length(feats))))
    t0 <- Sys.time()
    fit <- randomForest(
      x     = train_raw[, feats, drop = FALSE],
      y     = train_raw$label,
      ntree = ntree,
      mtry  = mtry
    )
    cat(sprintf("    %-10s %d features, mtry=%d, %.1fs\n",
                tier, length(feats), mtry,
                as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))))
    by_tier[[tier]] <- fit
  }
  saveRDS(list(by_tier = by_tier, fp = fp), path)
  by_tier
}

rf_teachers <- refit_rf_for_surrogate()
  [surrogate] refitting RF teacher on Lexical, FullLite ...
    Lexical    13 features, mtry=3, 36.1s
    FullLite   34 features, mtry=5, 18.7s

8.2 Surrogate-tree grid search on fidelity

The grid is small by design. We tune the three knobs that actually change how faithfully a rpart tree can reproduce a decision surface. maxdepth bounds the tree’s expressiveness, cp sets the pruning threshold that decides which splits are retained, minbucket forces leaves to be large enough to resist fitting noise in the teacher’s predictions.

Fidelity is the overall metric we optimise on the held-out test set:

\[\text{fidelity} = \frac{1}{n_\text{test}} \sum_i \mathbf{1}\bigl[\hat{y}^{\text{tree}}_i = \hat{y}^{\text{RF}}_i\bigr].\]

The rest of this report (6.1.1, 7) judges classifiers on Sensitivity / Specificity, not on overall accuracy, because a phishing-blocker’s two error types have asymmetric cost. For the same reason we decompose fidelity the same way: Sens vs RF is the share of RF-called phishing that the tree also calls phishing, Spec vs RF is the share of RF-called legit the tree also calls legit. These are the two numbers that tell us how the visualisation’s agreement with the model breaks down by class. We also report tree and RF Sens/Spec against the ground truth so the deployment-level operating point is visible.

tune_surrogate_tree <- function(tier, rf_fit,
                                maxdepths  = 3:7,
                                cps        = c(1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2),
                                minbuckets = c(10, 30, 100)) {
  feats <- tiers[[tier]]
  tr_x  <- train_raw[, feats, drop = FALSE]
  te_x  <- test_raw[ , feats, drop = FALSE]

  teacher_train <- predict(rf_fit, tr_x)
  teacher_test  <- predict(rf_fit, te_x)
  truth_test    <- test_raw$label

  # RF reference Sens/Spec on the test set - constant across the grid.
  rf_sens <- mean(teacher_test[truth_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
  rf_spec <- mean(teacher_test[truth_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

  tr_data <- tr_x
  tr_data$teacher_label <- teacher_train

  rows  <- list()
  trees <- list()
  for (md in maxdepths) for (cpv in cps) for (mb in minbuckets) {
    key <- sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", md, cpv, mb)
    tree <- rpart(
      teacher_label ~ ., data = tr_data,
      method  = "class",
      model   = TRUE,
      control = rpart.control(maxdepth = md, cp = cpv,
                              minbucket = mb, xval = 0)
    )
    pr_class <- predict(tree, te_x, type = "class")
    pr_prob  <- predict(tree, te_x, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
    n_leaves <- sum(tree$frame$var == "<leaf>")

    # Overall agreement - Task 4's fidelity metric.
    fidelity <- mean(pr_class == teacher_test)

    # Per-class agreement: the Sens/Spec decomposition of fidelity,
    # treating the RF prediction as the tree's training target.
    sens_vs_rf <- mean(pr_class[teacher_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
    spec_vs_rf <- mean(pr_class[teacher_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

    # Deployment-level operating point: tree vs. actual truth.
    tree_sens <- mean(pr_class[truth_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
    tree_spec <- mean(pr_class[truth_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

    auc <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(truth_test, pr_prob,
                                levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
    rows[[key]] <- tibble(
      tier = tier, maxdepth = md, cp = cpv, minbucket = mb,
      leaves = n_leaves,
      fidelity   = fidelity,
      sens_vs_rf = sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf = spec_vs_rf,
      tree_sens  = tree_sens,  tree_spec  = tree_spec,
      rf_sens    = rf_sens,    rf_spec    = rf_spec,
      auc        = auc
    )
    trees[[key]] <- tree
  }
  list(results = bind_rows(rows), trees = trees,
       teacher_test = teacher_test, truth_test = truth_test,
       rf_sens = rf_sens, rf_spec = rf_spec)
}

load_surrogate <- function(tier, rf_fit) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS,
                    paste0("surrogate_", tolower(tier), ".rds"))
  fp <- digest::digest(list(
    tier       = tier,
    features   = sort(tiers[[tier]]),
    n_train    = nrow(train_raw),
    rf_ntree   = rf_fit$ntree,
    tree_store = "model=TRUE",
    schema     = "v2_sens_spec"
  ))
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    if (isTRUE(cached$fp == fp)) return(cached$data)
  }
  cat(sprintf("  [surrogate-%s] tuning rpart grid...\n", tier))
  t0 <- Sys.time()
  data <- tune_surrogate_tree(tier, rf_fit)
  cat(sprintf("    done in %.1fs (%d combinations)\n",
              as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs")),
              nrow(data$results)))
  saveRDS(list(data = data, fp = fp), path)
  data
}

surr_lex  <- load_surrogate("Lexical",  rf_teachers[["Lexical"]])
  [surrogate-Lexical] tuning rpart grid...
    done in 6.6s (45 combinations)
surr_full <- load_surrogate("FullLite", rf_teachers[["FullLite"]])
  [surrogate-FullLite] tuning rpart grid...
    done in 8.0s (45 combinations)
all_surr  <- bind_rows(surr_lex$results, surr_full$results)

8.3 Fidelity vs. depth

The fidelity saturation curve - best fidelity per (tier, maxdepth) across all (cp, minbucket) - tells us how much tree expressiveness is needed before further depth stops buying alignment with the RF. RF reference AUCs come from 5.1.

rf_ref <- tibble(
  tier    = c("Lexical", "FullLite"),
  rf_auc  = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_auc,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_auc
  ),
  rf_sens = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_sens,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_sens
  ),
  rf_spec = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_spec,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_spec
  )
)

depth_tbl <- all_surr %>%
  group_by(tier, maxdepth) %>%
  slice_max(fidelity, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  left_join(rf_ref, by = "tier") %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier, maxdepth) %>%
  transmute(Tier = tier, MaxDepth = maxdepth, Leaves = leaves,
            Fidelity     = round(fidelity,   4),
            `Sens vs RF` = round(sens_vs_rf, 4),
            `Spec vs RF` = round(spec_vs_rf, 4),
            `Tree AUC`   = round(auc,        4),
            `RF AUC`     = round(rf_auc,     4))

kable(depth_tbl,
      caption = "Best surrogate per (tier, maxdepth) on the S6 test set. Fidelity = overall tree/RF agreement; Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF = its per-class decomposition (treating RF prediction as target). Winning (cp, minbucket) picked by fidelity; ties broken by fewer leaves.")
Best surrogate per (tier, maxdepth) on the S6 test set. Fidelity = overall tree/RF agreement; Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF = its per-class decomposition (treating RF prediction as target). Winning (cp, minbucket) picked by fidelity; ties broken by fewer leaves.
Tier MaxDepth Leaves Fidelity Sens vs RF Spec vs RF Tree AUC RF AUC
Lexical 3 6 0.9345 0.9756 0.8693 0.8265 0.9687
Lexical 4 10 0.9523 0.9834 0.9030 0.8366 0.9687
Lexical 5 16 0.9570 0.9861 0.9107 0.8370 0.9687
Lexical 6 17 0.9672 0.9766 0.9521 0.8557 0.9687
Lexical 7 36 0.9698 0.9870 0.9426 0.8564 0.9687
FullLite 3 7 0.9645 0.9700 0.9589 0.9835 0.9999
FullLite 4 11 0.9777 0.9744 0.9810 0.9858 0.9999
FullLite 5 16 0.9857 0.9877 0.9836 0.9931 0.9999
FullLite 6 21 0.9897 0.9867 0.9927 0.9919 0.9999
FullLite 7 25 0.9903 0.9883 0.9923 0.9926 0.9999

The 8.3 table reports the fidelity ceiling - the best rpart can do at each depth, regardless of readability. The Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF columns show how the remaining disagreement splits across the two classes: a fidelity of 0.96 with Sens-vs-RF 0.97 and Spec-vs-RF 0.95 means the tree disagrees with the RF mostly on the legit side.

For the plotted diagrams in 8.4 we pick a different tree: the surrogate with the highest fidelity under a readability cap of =< 15 leaves. A 37-leaf tree technically has a higher fidelity number, but it is visually unparseable on a slide. The price for the readability cap is small: fidelity drops by ~0.009 on Lexical (from 0.967 with 37 leaves to 0.958 with 14 leaves) and by ~0.003 on FullLite (from 0.990 to 0.987 with 15 leaves). The deepest tree levels buy almost no extra agreement with the RF.

MAX_PLOT_LEAVES <- 15L

winners <- all_surr %>%
  filter(leaves <= MAX_PLOT_LEAVES) %>%
  group_by(tier) %>%
  slice_max(fidelity, n = 1, with_ties = TRUE) %>%
  slice_min(leaves,   n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

winners %>%
  mutate(across(c(fidelity, sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf,
                  tree_sens, tree_spec, rf_sens, rf_spec, auc),
                ~ round(.x, 4))) %>%
  select(tier, maxdepth, cp, minbucket, leaves,
         fidelity, sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf,
         tree_sens, tree_spec, rf_sens, rf_spec, auc) %>%
  kable(caption = sprintf("Plotted surrogate tree per tier: highest fidelity subject to leaves <= %d. Ties broken by fewer leaves. `tree_sens`/`tree_spec` are vs. ground truth; `rf_sens`/`rf_spec` are the RF reference - the closer they are, the less the surrogate changes the operating point.", MAX_PLOT_LEAVES))
Plotted surrogate tree per tier: highest fidelity subject to leaves <= 15. Ties broken by fewer leaves. tree_sens/tree_spec are vs. ground truth; rf_sens/rf_spec are the RF reference - the closer they are, the less the surrogate changes the operating point.
tier maxdepth cp minbucket leaves fidelity sens_vs_rf spec_vs_rf tree_sens tree_spec rf_sens rf_spec auc
FullLite 6 1e-03 10 15 0.9873 0.9870 0.9877 0.9857 0.9850 0.9970 0.9957 0.9913
Lexical 7 1e-04 100 15 0.9595 0.9606 0.9577 0.9377 0.7263 0.9697 0.7427 0.8617

8.4 Winning trees

key_lex <- winners %>% filter(tier == "Lexical") %>%
  transmute(key = sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", maxdepth, cp, minbucket)) %>%
  pull(key)
tree_lex <- surr_lex$trees[[key_lex]]

if (has_rpart_plot) {
  rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
    tree_lex, type = 2, extra = 104, fallen.leaves = TRUE,
    box.palette = c("#D73027", "#1A9850"),
    main = sprintf("Lexical surrogate (fidelity %.3f vs RF)",
                   winners$fidelity[winners$tier == "Lexical"])
  )
} else {
  plot(tree_lex, uniform = TRUE, margin = 0.12,
       main = "Lexical surrogate tree")
  text(tree_lex, use.n = TRUE, cex = 0.65)
}

key_full <- winners %>% filter(tier == "FullLite") %>%
  transmute(key = sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", maxdepth, cp, minbucket)) %>%
  pull(key)
tree_full <- surr_full$trees[[key_full]]

if (has_rpart_plot) {
  rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
    tree_full, type = 2, extra = 104, fallen.leaves = TRUE,
    box.palette = c("#D73027", "#1A9850"),
    main = sprintf("FullLite surrogate (fidelity %.3f vs RF)",
                   winners$fidelity[winners$tier == "FullLite"])
  )
} else {
  plot(tree_full, uniform = TRUE, margin = 0.12,
       main = "FullLite surrogate tree")
  text(tree_full, use.n = TRUE, cex = 0.65)
}

RF variable importance cross-check

The surrogate’s root split is a claim about what the RF thinks is the most discriminative single feature. To see whether that claim is faithful to the teacher - rather than an artefact of the one tree we plotted - we read the RF’s own variable importance for the same teacher and compare the ordering against the tree’s top few splits.

# base-R title() goes through utf8towcs() which chokes on em-dashes
# in non-UTF-8 Windows locales - we keep the surrounding prose
# em-dashes intact but use a plain hyphen in the plot titles here.
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 7, 3, 1))
randomForest::varImpPlot(
  rf_teachers[["Lexical"]],
  n.var = min(10, length(tiers[["Lexical"]])),
  main  = "RF variable importance - Lexical"
)
randomForest::varImpPlot(
  rf_teachers[["FullLite"]],
  n.var = 10,
  main  = "RF variable importance - FullLite (top 10)"
)

par(op)

If the feature at the tree’s root is also in the top few rows of the RF importance plot for the same tier, the surrogate isn’t improvising - it’s picking up the same signal ranking the RF itself assigns. That is the evidence that lets us read the tree diagram as a visualisation of RF behaviour rather than a parallel classifier that happens to agree on the test set.

8.5 What the surrogate does and doesn’t capture

What the surrogate visualises. RF and the tree both work by axis-aligned splits, and on each tier the surrogate’s root variable is also varImpPlot’s top-ranked one.

  • Lexical. Root is NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL < 3. The high-special-char branch (~70 % of rows, mostly phish) refines through NoOfDegitsInURL, NoOfSubDomain, TLDLength and CharContinuationRate; the other branch splits on URLLength, then NoOfSubDomain and NoOfLettersInURL.
  • FullLite. Root is HasSocialNet = 1. The top three layers are trust-tier binaries (HasCopyrightInfo, HasDescription, IsHTTPS, HasSubmitButton); URL counts only show up deeper, refining leaves the trust flags could not split. Once page-level flags are available, RF leans on them first and uses the URL features only as a tiebreaker.

So the diagram does reflect what RF actually keys on, not just a second classifier that happens to agree on the test set.

What the surrogate cannot capture. RF averages 300 bagged trees, each split seeing only mtry = (sqrt)p features, which captures 3+ feature interactions a single tree cannot. That cost is visible on Lexical: Tree AUC 0.862 vs. RF AUC 0.969 (gap 0.107), and Sens drops 3.3 pp (0.968 → 0.934) when the surrogate stands in for RF. Spec only loses 1.0 pp. On FullLite both shifts are ~1 pp because the RF advantage over single-rule classifiers is gone there. So the Lexical surrogate is a structural picture of RF, not a deployable replacement for it.

Parameters and readability cap. maxdepth is the binding lever: below 3 the surrogate underfits on Lexical (fidelity ~0.85), past 5–6 it plateaus. cp = 10^-4 is permissive enough that depth, not pruning, sets the ceiling, and minbucket >= 10 keeps leaves stable against teacher-label noise. The plotted trees in 8.4 add a =< 15-leaf cap so the diagram stays readable. This costs ~0.009 fidelity on Lexical and ~0.003 on FullLite.

Per-class fidelity. Sens vs RF and Spec vs RF sit within a percent of each other on both tiers (Lexical 0.957 / 0.962, FullLite 0.987 / 0.985), so the surrogate isn’t just tracking the easy class. At depth 3 on Lexical the symmetry breaks - Spec vs RF falls to ~0.87 while Sens vs RF holds at ~0.98 - and that 11 pp gap closes again by depth 6–7. Shallower diagrams therefore show only one side of RF’s behaviour.
